title: 2026/03/05 EDSI
displayTitle: 2026-03-04 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
description: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill Retail NZ Carolyn Young, Chief Executive Denise Garland, Manager Centre for International Corporate Tax Accountability and Research (CICTAR) Edward Miller, Researcher Fired Up Stilettos Bianca Beebe, Organiser Russell McVeagh Bradley Aburn, Partner Petra Carey, Partner Troy Pilkington, Partner Debbie Port
descriptionSummary: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill. Retail NZ. Carolyn Young, Chief Executive

chunks.text: So we have tail and Z first. Yeah. Thanks Caroline.

Thank you so much. When you're ready. Yeah. So we have 10 minutes with you and you know how it works. Yeah, thank you. Um, Marlina, it's Carolyn Young, chief Executive of Retail nz, and my colleague, I'm Denise Garland. I'm the advocacy manager at Retail nz. Look, thank you very much for the opportunity to come in and speak, and I think, um, you know, this is a really complex area in terms of the retail space and I, and, and you know, there's a bit of a push pull factor that we see amongst our members.

Our members, you know, go from really small individual stores to large national stores and some of which are multinational. Mm-hmm. And so the challenges that we have seen really, um, New Zealand is a diverse country, and we are spread over three islands, um, two long skinny islands where we mostly live around the outskirts.

And distributing goods and services across the country can be challenging and it can be expensive. And those things mean that, um, getting price, uh, it gives price pressure and getting, you know, um, competitive pricing can be quite challenging, especially if you're smaller. So there are some advantages in the things that we've seen from some smaller businesses being able to collaborate and be able to negotiate collectively.

And there are some, um, some advantages of bigger businesses also having those, um, abilities. And then if you look at a position that we're in right now and some feedback, we've been going out to members to ask what the impact of the current situation with Iran could be. As an example to think about in your considerations, larger businesses, um, have said to us that they are, uh, likely to see some price increases from suppliers, which they'll absorb as much of those price pressures that they can until it gets to a point

where they would need to pass those on. So, um, and this is something that we see quite common. It's quite common for a larger business to absorb supplier price increase before anything is passed on further down the track. With a smaller business, they don't have that ability, even if they're buying sort of in a collective space.

So we do need some big businesses to be able to give competitive pricing in New Zealand and understanding it's only 5 million people. So I think there's kind of two different things. Yeah. And I think, um, as well, like, I mean, retail NZ sees the benefits in those in collaboration spaces and, and from the different sizes.

Um, I guess there's different views as to what would benefit of the, the purchasing power of small businesses being able to collaborate, but also in terms of things like sustainability, recycling schemes is sort of where, um, our bigger members would see benefits in, in supporting that kind of, um, collaboration space.

Um, there was also a, a bit of weariness as well amongst our members regarding the, um, merger regime, um, proposed changes just in terms of clarity over what some of the definitions were. And also noting that the changes in Australia have only just come into place and that we, they haven't really been able to see how that will play out and sort of noting that perhaps caution is the best, um, best way go forward at this stage. Yeah. Mm-hmm.

Mm-hmm. So happy to take any Questions. Oh, thank you. Uh, questions. Yeah, thank you both. Um, Caroline, interested in, uh, your point around the collaboration. Do you, so do you think those roles will have, um, a disproportionately larger impact on the larger businesses? Is that what we are taking from this or, um, and I'm also interested in your view around the predatory pricing rule, whether you think that will have disproportionate effect on some of your larger members.

I think, um, it's a bit about caution. I think as we were saying is that, um, you need to be really careful that you don't say, uh, that every large business is, are disadvantaged to New Zealand and that they don't create competition or they don't provide good pricing because large businesses ha uh, have their own, um, their own market strategy around what it is they want to do and how they want to compete.

And so we don't want to put in place a regime that is going to think, they'll sit back and think, well, okay, well it doesn't, it's not advantageous for us to find the best pricing out there because we're gonna get hammered by other things. So I think it's about really understanding the balance that businesses are trying to get the best prices for New Zealand so they can get good market share, um, without, you know, coming in with a big stick.

So it is a real, a real thing about getting that balance and making sure that we don't, um, perceive that. I mean, if we take the supermarkets as an example, we have two major supermarket, well three major supermarkets in New Zealand, and there has been a lot of talk about trying to get a third or fourth one, you know, food stuff, north Island, south Island, and Woolworths.

Um, whilst everybody wants a another supermarket national chain to come to New Zealand, no one's stepping up. There's no nothing stopping someone coming in. So we have to acknowledge that supermarkets here are working hard to do the best they can. And I think when you look at, if you look, whether you're looking at hardware or, um, technology or any of those other areas, you've to really take into account that businesses are trying to do the best.

But there are some challenges in New Zealand's landscape. So it's a fine balance. It's time. Um, it's time. Oh, it's 10 minutes. Oh, sorry. 10 minutes. Yeah. So you a supplementary Yep. Um, interested in coming back to project racing, but can I ask you about what, um, in the merger rules is one of the things that has come up with your members?

The, um, voluntary undertakings, um, part of that regime? I guess that would be the thing that we would point to as one of the new parts of that. It's more like the Australian system. Your members will be familiar with this because they, they make voluntary undertakings with ir. Um, how, how has that gone for your Membership? It was more around sort of definitions around assets and the substantially less lessening competition in the market where sort of where they had their concerns as to how that might be viewed and exactly.

Um, so that's sort of where they were, um, most reluctant. And also the fact that, you know, there is case law out there already to guide New Zealand, um, in terms of other situations where, where this may have been tested through there. So that sort of where, where that they had their reluctance in that space.

Mm-hmm. Um, just on the predatory pricing, Tess, I think, um, retail is an interesting example of where New Zealanders might really benefit from, um, having some clear rules, like some clearer rules I guess, around, um, overseas competitors coming, leading loss, leading for a long time in the market, um, backed by either private equity or, or big pockets.

Um, did, did you have any positive feedback about the introduction of, um, a erasing which was clearer under 36 c, um, to, you know, to make more credible prosecutions of competition from overseas To prevent that situation? Yes, that would be positive. And I think, you know, one of the big challenges that we do have in New Zealand right now is, um, New Zealand is buying offshore and the likes of Temu and Sheen and what that means for New Zealand economy and what it means for, you know, recycling and the longevity of goods and those sorts of things.

So, um, kind of slightly different, but definitely it's still predatory pricing when you think about, you know, what happens when you go onto those sites and, and who's lured in and where those products end up in New Zealand landscape. Mm-hmm. Hmm. All right. Good. Anymore? No. Looks like no more questions. Thank you so much for Thank, I appreciate your time.

Right. So next we have, um, so we should go to Ed Miller. Uh, yeah. All right. So we go to Center for International Corporate Text Accountability and Research, and he is on Zoom. Uh, thank you so much, Edward, for your submission. Thank you. Can you see and hear me?

Uh, we can see you and we can hear you as well. We have 10 minutes with you. So, uh, after you have spoken to your submission, if there is any time left, there might be questions from members. Thank you. Fantastic. Thanks very much. Well, um, my name's Edward Miller. I work for an organization called Sitar, where I undertake corporate research on a number of different markets in a number of different countries all around the world.

Um, thanks very much for the opportunity to submit. Um, and thanks to the committee for all the work that's been done in this space so far. It's really great to see positive reform in this sector. Um, like the Commerce Act, I was born in 1986 and like the Commerce Act, I'm losing hair in all the wrong places.

And I think it's great to see, um, this initiative to try and reform this legislation. I mean, unfortunately here in New Zealand, we, we kind of live now under the yoke of oligopoly power in many places. You know, we have non-competitive markets in, in banking, energy, grocery building materials, telecoms, insurance, a big chunk of workers and consumers weekly wage bill goes into the oligopoly sectors of the economy.

Um, in my prior role as the researcher at Workers First Union, we represent workers in a number of these sectors where they were, um, unfortunately on the receiving end of both, um, as consumers having to deal with non-competitive markets, um, for, um, fund, you know, getting access to basic goods and services, but also as workers being in non-competitive labor markets where there were very few employers, very little competition.

Um, whether you like unions or not, having a short, a small number of employers in the market, in the labor market means there's very little competition and there's very few protection measures for workers. So they end up being pushed towards unions more than they otherwise would. Um, we got here under the current act, we got to this position under the current act, which was kind of a product of that Chicago School of Regulation of competition, regula regulation, which put efficiency gains ahead of competition for consumers.

So regardless of how those efficiency gains were distributed, whether they went to workers, consumers, or whether they went to the, um, holders of businesses themselves passed on as as dividends to shareholders. And unfortunately, that is the situation that has happened, and it continues to happen a lot of the time.

I think that view is changing, and I think the reforms in this legislation are really helpful in getting us towards that. But there are a few things in the rules that, um, will keep us in that efficiency model, and I'll talk to those at the end. So I'm really glad to see the, um, targeting the problem of creeping acquisitions.

I saw this up close in a number of sectors in my previous work, particularly in the waste sector in which essentially there are now two companies that, um, run the vast majority of waste concessions around the country and environ waste and waste management, and also in the bus sector, which looks like there are a number of companies, but in fact there are essentially two Kinetic, which owns Go Bus and NZ Bus and Richie's, which is owned by KKR, the big, uh, New York investment firm based outta the Cayman Islands.

Um, since then I've looked at another, a number of other sectors like early childhood education, aged care diagnostic services, medical imaging services, primary care, dental care, all of these quite fundamental support services, uh, fundamental services, often services that are funded by the public purse in which this problem of, of creep creeping acquisitions by private equity funds has meant there is very little competition in the marketplace, and often tax dollars are being plunged into paying for services that are more expensive than they would

otherwise be because of those creeping acquisitions. So I want to applaud the work that's been done so far in this space. I think the three year aggregation rule is a really fantastic innovation, um, and will help slow the consolidation in some of those sectors and possibly create space for new market players. Um, there will always be a behavioral response by firms to new rules.

And, um, some of those, I'm sure private equity funds will start changing, um, exactly what their acquisition structures look like and how they do acquisitions when they do them timing wise. So there might be an argument to shift from a three year rule to a five year rule in the future, which probably aligns more tightly with what a private equity fund strategy would look like.

Um, but there's behavioral changes either way as well. The other behavioral response that you, we are likely to see and we've seen in Australia is that when the rules come in, then um, a whole lot of acquisitions are brought forward in terms of their existing timelines. So you probably end up with some poor decision making or poor deal making in the short term. Might not be a huge amount that we can do about that.

Uh, but there will be behavioral responses. Um, this, the new 47 E and 47 F powers look to be really strong, innovative, um, new rules that will make it easier for the commission to in investigate those non notified transactions. Um, hopefully this makes it a lot harder for mergers.

Um, that's substantially less in competition to fly under the radar, uh, but there is still a discovery gap and the commission will will be at a material disadvantage in terms of, uh, finding out about acquisitions that are taking place. Um, the comparison here to Australia is important. They've moved to this mandatory notification regime with full suspensory powers.

Um, the Australian view, or maybe the ac CC view was that the voluntary regime is essentially asking a shark to report itself to a lifeguard. Um, but once a shark has taken a chunk out of a swim, it's very hard to stitch that back on. So sewing things back together is a lot more difficult.

And having those mandatory powers to suspend, um, or to suspend all of those non notified transactions would give the commission a bit more of an opportunity to deal with the material disadvantages that they face in terms of being able to engage with all of the acquisitions that are taking place or all of the mergers.

Um, so, you know, it's a design question more than anything, and I think it would be interesting to get more feedback from the ACCC on how the implementation is, is looking there. Uh, the predatory pricing changes I think are really fantastic. Um, and I wanna applaud the work that's being done in this space, moving, um, from that purpose test, which is next to impossible to prove, uh, purpose on the part of, um, predatory pricing to a more objective test is a really important one.

And it's a space where we are going a bit further than Australia as well. So it's great to see that, you know, we, we always seem to have, well, uh, for good reason, we always look to Australia, uh, that, that good reason being because so much of our economy is owned by Australian businesses, um, not just by the big monopoly operators, but also by, um, Australian domicile private equity funds.

So looking to Australia is important, but knowing when to go further than Australia is also really valuable. Um, and that's where I wanted to just wrap up by saying there are a couple of areas where I think this legislation could have gone further, and it would be great to go further in this respect. I think, you know, it's a bit of a missed opportunity in some respects, because this is, I guess the, the what the second major reform of the Commerce Act that we've had since it's inception.

Um, we are still stuck in the, I guess, efficiency remains the underlying, uh, legislative impulse within, within the proposed changes. And the, the evidence of that is that section 67 is still there as a kind of gateway test. You know, the efficiency goes in the competition machine if you like.

Uh, that's been left as is. So there are, the way the law operates, you have two, um, potential gateways. There's substantial lessening in competition, but sub, uh, mergers or acquisitions that substantially lessen competition can still proceed if they are deemed to, um, satisfy the efficiency defense if they result in public benefits.

And those public benefits do not need to. Um, there doesn't need to be any evidence of those public benefits really accrue to members of the public necessarily. So we can unfortunately, under six section 67 approve mergers that increase total welfare, um, or have efficiency gains regardless of where those efficiency gains end up, whether they're in the pockets of workers and consumers, or whether they're in the pockets of, uh, shareholders from the offshore owned firms that run much of the majority the monopoly economy.

So I think that's a major emission from the, the, um, reforms. And I think the second emission is that we don't have anything in this, these changes that will allow us to roll back the three or four decades of consolidation that we have had. We don't have new powers for the commission or for other, uh, regulators to be able to break up some of the oligopoly markets that we have and take us back to an era of greater competition.

And I think that's the important discussion moving forward. How do we roll back the failures of the past regime to move forward and make sure that all the great changes that are coming in, uh, that I've talked about, um, have the teeth to be able to benefit workers and consumers across the board. Uh, thank you so much. Uh, we have time for a very, very quick question, Dr.

Very quick David question. Thank you, ed. Very good. Uh, submission, um, we've, we've got a lot of people that tell us that predatory pricing is, um, has lots of unintended consequences, and there is no real evidence to support that there actually is predatory pricing. Do you have evidence? It's, it's a very hard thing to put together evidence in this space.

Um, I'm just for the, because we have so little time, I'm gonna say that the, the best people to have evidence are those people, the, the people that run firms who have been crushed by the predatory pricing. I'm not able to present evidence for you right now within the short space of time. David, Thank you, ed. All right. Thank you so much. Um, thanks, um, for your submission. Thank you. Thanks.
Uh, uh, I believe we have fired up still at those in the rules. Yeah. Thank you.
Is that Bianca? Yes, it is. Hi, Bianca. We have 10 minutes with you. Excellent. Thank you,

Uh, starting? Yep. Excellent. Thank you. Yes, honor. Uh, good morning committee. Thank you for inviting me to speak. My name is Bianca Bebe. I'm a public health researcher and chair of, uh, fired up stilettos, which advocates for the labor rights of independent contractors doing various kinds of adult entertainment and sex work in managed venues throughout New Zealand.

Um, so when fired up stilettos first came to Parliament in 2023, um, our petition asked for, among other things, the right to collectively bargain while maintaining our independent contracting status. Um, and in his official response to US Ministers Simpson, uh, did not commit the government to freestanding legislation, but said that he would consider us in changes to the Commerce Act and wanted to thank the government and Minister Simpson in particular, because that has clearly happened.

Um, the changes that we see to the Commerce Act will indeed allow us to go on the path to collectively bargaining while staying independent contractors. Um, but we have a few, um, suggestions that can give greater effect to the government's aims without risking, um, cartel behavior.

So clearly we strongly support the government's plan to implement a notification regime under Schedule eight. What we need is clarity on who can notify. Um, when we called the Commerce Commission, they said that in this legislation, it's unclear if each dancer at a venue would have to individually notify the commission, or if contractors could sign up with a legal entity who can then bargain on their behalf.

Uh, we wanna start using this legislation as soon as it's enacted. So ideally, as few bureaucratic roadblocks as possible would be great. The ideal scenario would be for independent contractors to have the freedom to sign up with a legal entity, charity, or union, who can then notify the commission and bargain on behalf of its members.

Um, the streamlined process would reduce bureaucratic paperwork for the commission and encourage greater uptake among contractors, which will intend in turn, improve competition and working conditions in our sector. Uh, secondly, we'd like to discuss application fees to notify or be granted a class exemption. We know that application fees are not generally set in statutory law, but fees have the power to make or break this legislation.

Uh, the best legislation in the world is not useful if the price to access it is out of reach of those who actually need it. Um, we note that the legislation gives the commerce commission the discretion to waive, um, or reduce fees if they would present a quote undue hardship. Um, but this solution would likely present even more problems because the things that can lead to undue hardship in our industry are frequently inappropriate to discuss with people who are untrained in matters of sexual harm.

So it would instead make much more sense for there to be no application fee for anyone applying under Schedule eight, since we are, by definition, relatively low revenue contractors who do not pose a cartel risk through bargaining collectively. And finally, I wanna talk about retaliation and victimization, which is the most important aspect.

I cannot possibly overstate the level of impunity with which venues have grown accustomed to operating. When dancers have tried to negotiate with clubs to remove even the most egregious and exploitative clauses from their contracts, clubs, love to just smirk in our faces and say, why would we talk to you girls? Turn 18 every day.

Multiple dancers have been explicitly told by venue management that they would be fired if they even followed, fired up stilettos on their Instagram accounts, or liked one of our posts, particularly in places like Auckland and Wellington with a high student population combined with a high cost of living and a dearth of jobs that accommodate a student's schedule.

Clubs have evolved an entire business model around creating extractive predatory conditions for contracted workers and immediately firing them if they object, because they know there's always a new teenager who needs to pay rent. If there is no obligation for these venues to act in good faith, they will not. If this legislation were to pass in its current form, independent contractors would be able to notify the Commerce Commission of their intent to collectively bargain, and they would be immediately fired for doing so.

That means no one would actually use the law as intended, and the government's hard work would be wasted. We don't want that to happen. So we need the protections in place that will allow us to use this law. I wanna end by clarifying what our suggested changes will not do. They will not cause any conflict with the newly introduced gateway test of the Employment Relations Act or otherwise interfere with the government's aim to establish greater clarity on the difference between employees and contractors.

Fired up stilettos has been consistent in saying that we were hired as contractors and we would like to be treated as contractors. When we contractors are allowed to collectively negotiate our contracts, we will increase market competition and reduce the incentive for businesses to misclassify their workers in the first place. Our suggested changes will work in tandem with many other initiatives the government is currently working on rather than against 'em.

So to conclude, this notification regime is a great start, but it needs teeth. We encourage you to, uh, ensure the changes you're working so hard to achieve cannot simply be ignored by bad faith actors exploiting their market dominance to reduce competition, increase prices for consumers, and worsen working conditions for contractors.

So let's make sure the law applies equally to everyone. Thank you. Um, members, Christians, uh, dr. Questions, uh, Dr. Uh, thank you, Bianca. That was very clear and very well articulated, uh, in your submission, um, you talk about, um, the, the screen, uh, industry Workers Act of 22. Yes. Um, what is it particularly that you see that how this would work, um, in this instant if we were to, if we were able to make any amendments to this?

I'm not sure that we could, but mm-hmm. Um, do you think, how do you think that would be? So there's aspects of siwa that can be, uh, universally applied to any contractors who would be trying to seek an exemption under Schedule eight. So for instance, um, section 24 of siwa prohibits retaliatory termination or non-renewal of in individual contracts.

And Section 26 requires all parties to bargain in good faith. That is similar to what is currently in the proposed changes to the Commerce Act under 95 A and B. However, 90, or sorry 97 A and B, but 97 a specifies that employers cannot behave in a retaliatory fashion. And that is exactly the bind that we have been, um, getting into.

Right? We are not trying to be reclassified as employees, we want to be treated as contractors. Um, but our independence as contractors has not been respected at all. And indeed, our, uh, rights as people has not either. Um, another, uh, thing that could be useful, there is, uh, section, um, in, uh, CIWA, which obviously is for a specific, um, industry.

Um, we recognize that not all of it is gonna be applicable to this. There's just those two sections. And then, um, finally, section 72 says that parties that are un uh, unable to come to a consensus or face some kind of a dispute in their bargaining process, there needs to be some kind of third party that's able to break through when there is some kind of gridlock.

Um, the Commerce Commission, uh, in our initial hearings, um, acknowledge that many of the terms in our contracts are likely anti-competitive. Um, and they can see how we would have a substantial case, but we simply do not have the market power for them to take the time to go through a court process for what we need.

So they have said they're already overloaded and they can't help us. So what Ciwa did as a workaround of that on, uh, section 72, it said that parties that are in a dispute can apply to the ERA for determination of the dispute. Um, and that should be resolved, uh, uh, uh, in accordance with how the ERA would normally do between, um, bargaining employers and employees in that very limited context.

Not in order to reclassify or misclassify people, but simply to have a third party that is equipped for that specific dynamic, um, which can be applied appropriately even while maintaining our independent contracting status. Mm-hmm. Um, Ricardo, thank you so much, Bianca, that was such a clear submission on page one.

You noted, um, some of the problems with the status of school. I know you talked about some of the stuff in relationship to the student population, but what is the end goal here and what are you, what kind of behavior are you trying to stamp out? You talked a little bit about fines, for example, in, um, your first page, but would you mind expanding a bit of why you're coming here asking for these changes, and what's the problem with the status quo more broadly?

Absolutely. Um, the sort of things that we're up against, uh, the educational workforce committee when we previously, um, submitted, acknowledged that they can't think of a single other industry that faces, um, explicit clauses in their contracts as we do. So the most egregious one, I think there's a system of fines, um, for, uh, punitive, basically punitive fines that are in contracts.

The most egregious one, I would argue is a $500 fine and forfeiture of 50% of the knight's tips for rudeness to management as determined by management. Um, then also many clubs implement explicit non-compete clauses that extend not only while contracting at a particular club, but also after the business relationship has been terminated.

I included in our written submission a picture of, uh, the mermaids contract, which has a two year non-competition clause. So it's, uh, a quite an irony that we find ourselves in, right where, uh, preventing collective bargaining, uh, among independent contractors is meant to promote competition and anti cartel behavior.

But what has happened is, uh, strip clubs have engaged in explicitly anti-competitive behavior, um, and, uh, concentrated the market to the extent that it has significantly increased prices for consumers and, uh, egregiously violated, um, uh, workplace relations and safety.
Thank you so much. Um, thank you so much, Bianca, for your submission and thank you for answering those questions. Thank you. Um, next we have Russell, if, are they here on Zoom? Yeah,

good morning. Thank you so much. We have, uh, the names I have is Bradley, Evan Petra, Kerry and Troy Kinon. Yep. Thank you so much for your submission. We have 10 minutes with you. Thank you. Morning. Um, thank you very much to the committee for having the opportunity to speak today.

Um, I'm Petra Kerry, a partner in Ross Russ Bay's competition and regulation team. And I'm joined here with Troy Ton in the middle and Bradley Aber. Um, in our allocated time, we would like to focus on the following three recommendations and our written submission. Firstly, Brad will speak to the removal of the predatory pricing proposal to avoid unintended consequences of higher prices for consumers, um, which has already been referenced this morning.

Um, and then my colleague Troy will speak on the, um, proposals to change the substantial lessening of competition test. And finally, time permitting. Um, I'll conclude with some supportive comments on the proposal for behavioral remedies. Um, and look, if we don't get there, just in, in very, um, short, uh, brief, we are really supportive of that proposal.

We consider them positive, uh, to an NZ Inc. Uh, subject to some important changes that we detail in our written submission, uh, to ensure they achieve the desire fit. Um, so I'll just pass on to Bradley to talk about pricing first. Thank you pet. And thank you to the committee for your time this morning. As pet mentioned, um, we are opposed to clause six of the bill that introduces a bright line prohibition on market power pricing below cost.

Firstly, we do acknowledge the practical reality that New Zealanders have been struggling with cost of living pressures and that getting the competition law settings right has an important role to play in both delivering competitive markets for the benefit of consumers and also addressing cost of living pressures, which many New Zealanders still face today.

However, in our opinion, the proposed introduction of a bright line prohibition on below cost pricing does not address these pressures, and in fact, is likely to have the opposite effect of higher prices for consumers. Firstly, we are concerned that the proposed changes to predatory pricing were not included in the comprehensive review or consultation that was undertaken by MDM to the Commerce Act.

There has been no evidence provided, and we are not aware of any evidence that predatory pricing is a widespread or practical concern in New Zealand. To the extent that there are isolated examples of predatory pricing, we consider that the current law, which was only recently amended in 2023, is robust enough to address anti-competitive below cost pricing.

So there is no evidential basis to suggest that a change is warranted in addition to there not being evidence that changes required. We have three main issues with the proposed new Bright Line prohibition. First bright line prohibitions are only appropriate where the relevant conduct is almost always harmful.

With respect to below cost pricing, it is clear that consumers benefit from this in the short run and in most cases benefit from such low prices in the long run. There are likely to be very few cases where below cost pricing is harmful. The economics literature is clear that predatory pricing is a very expensive way to deter competition and has a very low success rate.

This is why it is important that an actual impact on competition of the below cost pricing is undertaken as is required by the current law. The second concern we have with the new, uh, prohibition is, although there is an exception, that those exceptions for promotional pricing do not apply, where there's a pattern of behavior over a sustained period, now that carve out is a likely to prohibit routine behavior that consumers benefit from including where, um, businesses sell perishable or short dated stock below cost

to encourage consumption rather than wastage selling last year's or last season's models or stock at below cost to make room for new models selling goods below cost to charities or social groups to support charitable initiatives and employee discount schemes or arrangements. Third, we are concerned that the proposed introduction of this, um, deeming provision will have a chilling effect in our experience.

Businesses take their competition or obligations very seriously. The proposed prohibition relies on economic concepts that are susceptible to different interpretations and are not standard everyday accounting or financial concepts that businesses use to make everyday decisions.

Faced with the new Bright Line test businesses will err on the side of compliance, likely leading to higher prices across the economy. We do not consider that this is in the best interest of consumers or is consistent with the government's objective of going for growth. Our strong recommendation, therefore would be to delete clause six from the bill.

Now pass on to Troy. Thank you Brad, and good morning to the committee. I just wanna thank the committee for their work on this bill. We all recognize how important it is for New Zealand to get the competition or settings correct. And also just want to emphasize there are aspects of the bill we support some of the changes that are proposed to make it easier for businesses to collaborate in relation to positive initiatives such as collaboration, sustainability, supply chain initiatives, health and safety.

We really support the current authorization regime with the commission as cumbersome, burdensome, lengthy, costly. We just don't see it achieving what it needs to in those spaces. And we've certainly seen positive initiatives fall by the wayside because of that. So I encourage that. What I wanna focus on in my couple of minutes today is just the proposed change to the substantial lessening of competition standard.

In particular, the proposal that that gets changed across the entirety of the comac, including in relation to the behavioral aspects of the Commiss Act. I am very concerned about a proposal that would make New Zealand bespoke and different to Australia. The New Zealand market is fundamentally integrated with the Australian market.

There's a whole lot of benefits to New Zealanders from having a integrated, uh, closely aligned regulatory framework across Australia and New Zealand. A number of businesses operate across both those. They set their compliance regimes, their standards, et cetera, to reflect both those jurisdictions operations.
